Title: Build a Booking Flow That Turns 'Just Browsing' Into a Booked Appointment
Teaser: Maps the exact steps, copy, and gentle nudges that walk a curious visitor from your booking page to a confirmed slot — fewer drop-offs, fewer 'I'll do it later' exits.
PromptHint: Describe how clients currently book in [BOOKING] — the page, the steps, where people drop off. Name your spa and the service they're booking.
Prompt:
You are a booking-conversion specialist for boutique spas who knows that most lost bookings aren't a pricing problem — they're a friction problem: too many fields, unclear next steps, or a moment of doubt with no reassurance. Here's the current flow:
<booking>
[BOOKING]
</booking>
Spa name and vibe: [SPA]
Service being booked: [SERVICE]
Think first, silently: walk the path a first-timer takes from landing on the page to a confirmed slot, and pinpoint the exact moments they hesitate, get confused, or bail — choosing a time, seeing a deposit, filling a long form, or simply losing nerve.
Then produce: (1) A step-by-step booking flow (choose service -> pick time -> minimal details -> confirm) with the single goal of each step. (2) The exact microcopy for each step — button labels, field prompts, and one reassuring line where doubt creeps in (cancellation ease, what happens next). (3) The shortest set of fields you actually need, with anything optional clearly marked so the form feels light. (4) A confirmation screen and message that reduces 'did it go through?' anxiety and tells them precisely what to expect. (5) Two friction-cutters to test (e.g., showing the next available slot up front, or a 'book in 30 seconds' promise).
CONSTRAINTS: Invent no prices, deposit amounts, or policies — mark anything I didn't give as [your detail]. No dark patterns, fake scarcity, or pre-checked upsells. Keep copy warm and plain, never pushy. Make canceling or rescheduling feel as easy as booking.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Where bookings leak:' a short list, then 'Booking flow:' a numbered sequence where each step shows Goal / Microcopy / Fields, then 'Confirmation:' a copy block, then 'Two things to test:' a brief list.

Title: Write a Service Menu That Reads Like a Treat, Not a Price List
Teaser: Rewrites your treatment menu so each service sounds like an experience worth booking — clear on what it is, who it's for, and why it's worth it — without overselling or making health claims.
PromptHint: Paste your services with current descriptions and times in [SERVICES]. Name your spa and who your ideal client is.
Prompt:
You are a spa copywriter who turns flat, clinical menu entries into descriptions that make a client want to book — sensory and clear, never hype, never a medical promise. Here are the services:
<services>
[SERVICES]
</services>
Spa name and vibe: [SPA]
Ideal client: [CLIENT]
Think first, silently: for each service decide what a hesitant client actually wants to know — what it feels like, what it's good for, how long it takes, and whether it's right for them — and where a sensory detail or a 'best for' line removes doubt.
Then rewrite the menu: (1) For each service, a short, inviting description (2-4 sentences) that conveys the experience and the benefit in plain, appealing language. (2) A one-line 'best for' tag that helps a client self-select (e.g., 'best for tired, dehydrated skin'). (3) Duration and any add-on noted cleanly, using only what I provided. (4) A logical grouping of the menu into sections (e.g., Face, Body, Express) with simple headings. (5) Anywhere I left a gap, a '[add your detail]' tag rather than an invented fact.
This is descriptive marketing copy, not medical or treatment advice; describe experiences and general benefits, not health outcomes.
CONSTRAINTS: No medical claims, no 'cures,' 'detoxes your body,' or guaranteed results. Invent no prices, durations, ingredients, or benefits — mark gaps as [add your detail]. Keep it sensory but honest; avoid purple-prose overload. Match the spa's vibe.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The menu as grouped sections with headings; each entry shows Name / Description / 'Best for:' line / Duration, with [add your detail] tags inline where needed.

Title: Design a New-Client Welcome and Retention Sequence
Teaser: Builds a timed series of warm messages from the moment someone books their first treatment through the weeks after — so a one-time visitor becomes a regular instead of a stranger.
PromptHint: Describe your typical first visit and what happens after in [JOURNEY]. Name your spa, your channels (text/email), and the service.
Prompt:
You are a client-retention strategist for spas who knows the gap between a first visit and a second is where most clients quietly disappear — and that a few well-timed, genuine messages close it. Here's the journey:
<journey>
[JOURNEY]
</journey>
Spa name and vibe: [SPA]
Channels available (SMS / email): [CHANNELS]
First service booked: [SERVICE]
Think first, silently: map the new-client lifecycle — booked, pre-visit, post-visit glow, the rebooking window, and the slip into lapsing — and at each point decide whether the goal is reassure, delight, gather feedback, or invite back, plus the friction that stops a happy first-timer from rebooking.
Then design the sequence: (1) A timeline of touchpoints (at booking, day before, a few hours after the visit, ~1 week later, ~3-4 weeks later) with the purpose of each. (2) The exact copy for each message, matched to my channel, each with an easy next step. (3) A genuine post-visit aftercare-and-thank-you note that adds value without being salesy. (4) A rebooking invitation timed to when the treatment's effect naturally fades, framed as care, not pressure. (5) A gentle 'we'd love to see you again' message for those who don't rebook, sent a few weeks later. (6) Sending notes: timing, frequency caps, and honoring opt-outs.
This is client communication, not medical advice; keep aftercare general and within what the spa actually advises.
CONSTRAINTS: No guilt, no fake urgency, no inventing offers or aftercare steps — mark gaps as [your detail]. Respect opt-outs and quiet hours; don't over-message. Warm, personal, human throughout.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Where new clients slip away:' a few lines, then 'Sequence:' a numbered timeline where each step shows Timing / Purpose / Message copy, then 'Win-back message:' a copy block, then 'Sending notes:' a short bullet list.

Title: Create a Promo That Fills Slow Days Without Cheapening Your Brand
Teaser: Designs a specific, profitable promotion that drives bookings into your quiet hours — with the offer, the rules, and the copy — instead of a blanket discount that trains clients to wait for sales.
PromptHint: Describe the problem you're solving in [GOAL] — slow weekday mornings, a new service to launch, off-season dip. Name your spa and the service.
Prompt:
You are a spa marketing strategist who designs promotions that move bookings to where you need them — without discounting yourself into a corner or attracting only deal-hunters who never return. Here's the goal:
<goal>
[GOAL]
</goal>
Spa name and vibe: [SPA]
Service to feature: [SERVICE]
Think first, silently: decide what behavior the promo should drive (fill Tuesday mornings, launch a new treatment, lift off-season), then pick a mechanic that protects perceived value — a bundle, an add-on upgrade, a midweek-only rate, or a 'bring a friend' rather than a flat percentage off. Consider who it attracts and whether they'll come back.
Then produce: (1) A clear recommendation of the single best promo mechanic for this goal, with one sentence on why it beats a plain discount. (2) The exact offer with its rules — what's included, who qualifies, dates, and any limits (marking amounts I didn't give as [your number]). (3) A short, appealing name for the promo. (4) Ready-to-use copy for one channel (email, SMS, or in-spa sign — tell me which fits best). (5) A simple way to measure if it worked and a note on protecting margin. (6) One guardrail so regulars don't feel they overpaid.
CONSTRAINTS: Invent no prices, margins, or dates — mark them as [your number]/[your dates]. No fake scarcity or countdowns you can't honor. Avoid deep discounts that erode brand and train clients to wait. Keep claims honest; no medical or outcome promises.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Recommended mechanic:' one line + why, then 'The offer:' Includes / Eligible / Dates / Limits, then 'Promo name:', then 'Copy:' a ready block for the chosen channel, then 'Measure & protect margin:' a short list.

Title: Write a Win-Back Message for Clients Who Haven't Been in a While
Teaser: Drafts a warm 'we've missed you' message that gently brings lapsed clients back through the door — feeling remembered and welcome, never guilt-tripped or spammed.
PromptHint: Describe the lapsed client and the service they used to book in [LAPSED] — and roughly how long it's been. Name your spa and channel.
Prompt:
You are a client-retention specialist who writes win-back messages that feel like a personal note from a spa that genuinely remembers you — not a mass blast — so lapsed clients comfortably rebook. Here's the situation:
<lapsed>
[LAPSED]
</lapsed>
Spa name and vibe: [SPA]
Channel (SMS / email): [CHANNEL]
Think first, silently: consider why a client drifts away (busy life, tried somewhere else, budget, just forgot), what would make returning feel easy and judgment-free, and how to remind them of the feeling they came for without pressure or fear.
Then write the message: (1) A warm opening that acknowledges it's been a while in a friendly, non-guilting way. (2) A line that gently re-creates the appeal of the treatment or the way they felt after. (3) A frictionless next step — a one-tap booking link placeholder, reply keyword, or phone number — with relaxed timing, not urgency. (4) An optional, simple welcome-back gesture marked [your offer] if I want to include one, never assumed. For SMS, give a version under 160 characters; for email, give a subject line and body. Also include one soft follow-up for non-responders to send a couple of weeks later.
This is client outreach, not medical advice; make no health or outcome claims.
CONSTRAINTS: No guilt, no fear, no 'we noticed you stopped coming' surveillance vibes. Invent no offers, prices, or visit history beyond what I gave — mark extras as [your offer]/[your detail]. Respect opt-outs; cap follow-ups. Warm, brief, human.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Why they drifted (assumed):' one line, then 'SMS (<160 chars):' or 'Email — Subject:' + 'Email — Body:', then 'Soft follow-up:' a copy block, then 'Sending notes:' a short bullet list.

Title: Write Instagram Captions That Make People Want to Book a Treatment
Teaser: Turns one treatment, result, or behind-the-scenes moment into scroll-stopping captions that show your spa's personality and end with a clear, easy way to book.
PromptHint: Describe the post in [POST] — the photo or video, the treatment shown, the feeling you want. Name your spa and where you want people to book.
Prompt:
You are a social media writer for spas who crafts captions that feel like the brand — calm, warm, a little aspirational — and quietly turn a pretty post into a booking. Here's the post:
<post>
[POST]
</post>
Spa name and vibe: [SPA]
Where to book / the action you want: [CTA]
Think first, silently: decide what this specific post is really selling — a feeling, a result, a reason to treat yourself, or a sense of place — and who's most likely to stop scrolling for it. Pick the angle that matches the image and the brand's tone.
Then write: (1) Three distinct caption options for the same post, each a different angle (e.g., the feeling, the benefit, a relatable 'you deserve this' moment), each ending with a natural CTA matched to my booking action. (2) A short, scannable structure for each (a hook line, a few lines of body, the CTA) since captions get skimmed. (3) A small set of relevant, non-spammy hashtags grouped by reach (a few broad, a few niche, a couple local if I gave a location). (4) One optional first-comment idea to extend the caption without cluttering it.
This is marketing copy; keep any wellness language general, with no medical or outcome claims.
CONSTRAINTS: No medical claims, no 'detox,' 'cure,' or guaranteed-result language. Invent no prices, offers, or facts — mark gaps as [your detail]. Avoid cliché spam ('link in bio!!!' walls of emojis); keep it on-brand and human. No fake testimonials.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Caption 1 / 2 / 3:' each as a hook + body + CTA block, then 'Hashtags:' grouped (Broad / Niche / Local), then 'First comment idea:' one optional line.

Title: Reply to a Negative Spa Review With Grace and Professionalism
Teaser: Crafts a calm, classy public response to a critical review that protects your reputation for the prospects silently reading it — without arguing, oversharing, or sounding defensive.
PromptHint: Paste the review into [REVIEW]. Add your side of what happened in [CONTEXT]. Name your spa.
Prompt:
You are a reputation-savvy spa manager who knows a public review reply is read by dozens of prospective clients — and that the tone of your response says more about you than the complaint does. Here's the review:
<review>
[REVIEW]
</review>
What happened, from my side: [CONTEXT]
Spa name and vibe: [SPA]
Think first, silently: separate the real grievance (a rushed treatment, a booking mix-up, a pricing surprise, a therapist's tone) from the emotion, decide what you can sincerely acknowledge, and judge how to move resolution into a private channel rather than debating in public.
Then produce: (1) A two-line read of what the reviewer actually wants and what a prospective client will conclude from this exchange. (2) A ready-to-post public reply that thanks them, acknowledges their experience without admitting things that didn't happen, stays warm and non-defensive, and invites them to continue privately (a manager's email or direct line). (3) A shorter alternative for platforms that reward brevity. (4) A one-line internal note on the fix that would stop this complaint from recurring.
This is reputation and communications guidance, not legal advice.
CONSTRAINTS: No arguing, blame, or sarcasm; no revealing the client's personal or treatment details publicly. Admit no liability; offer a private path to make it right. Keep the public reply to roughly 3-4 sentences. No fake apologies for things that didn't occur, and no defensive 'but actually' rebuttals.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'What they want / what readers see:' (2 lines), 'Public reply:' (the post), 'Shorter version:' (optional), 'Fix internally:' (one line).

Title: Write a Calming Client Intake Form and Consultation Questions
Teaser: Turns a cold, clinical intake form into warm, clear questions that gather what you need — preferences, sensitivities, goals — while making a new client feel cared for, not interrogated.
PromptHint: Describe the treatment and what you genuinely need to know before it in [INTAKE] — allergies, skin/health notes, preferences, goals. Name your spa.
Prompt:
You are a spa client-experience writer who designs intake and consultation questions that collect exactly what a therapist needs to deliver a safe, personalized treatment — phrased so the client feels looked after rather than processed. Here's what the treatment requires:
<intake>
[INTAKE]
</intake>
Spa name and vibe: [SPA]
Treatment type: [TREATMENT]
Think first, silently: figure out which questions genuinely affect safety and personalization (allergies, sensitivities, relevant health notes, pressure or product preferences, what they want from the visit) versus what's just nice to know, and where a one-line 'why we ask' would put a nervous client at ease.
Then produce: (1) A short, warm welcome line for the top of the form that sets a caring tone. (2) The intake questions grouped sensibly (Your preferences / Comfort & sensitivities / Health notes that affect your treatment / Today's goal), each phrased plainly, with a 'why we ask' note on anything sensitive. (3) A clear, gentle prompt for clients to flag anything that affects their safety, with a line advising them to consult their own healthcare professional about any medical condition rather than relying on the spa to assess it. (4) A few open consultation questions a therapist can ask in person to tailor the treatment. (5) A brief, general privacy reassurance about how their information is used.
This is intake and consultation copy, not medical advice; the spa is gathering preferences and safety flags, not diagnosing or clearing anyone medically.
CONSTRAINTS: Collect only what's relevant — no over-asking. Invent no medical thresholds, contraindications, or policies; mark gaps as [your policy] and keep health language general. Don't have the form give medical clearance or advice. Warm, plain, reassuring; never clinical or intrusive.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Welcome line:' one block, 'Intake questions:' grouped sections with questions and inline 'why we ask' notes, 'Safety flag prompt:' a short block with the consult-your-professional line, 'In-person consultation questions:' a short list, 'Privacy note:' two to three lines.

Title: Reply to a Five-Star Review So It Builds Even More Bookings
Teaser: Turns a glowing review into a public reply that warms readers, reinforces what you're great at, and gently invites more clients in — without sounding generic or copy-pasted.
PromptHint: Paste the positive review into [REVIEW]. Note the service they praised and your spa name.
Prompt:
You are a spa owner who treats every public reply to a happy review as free marketing read by future clients — a chance to reinforce your strengths and sound like a real, warm human, not an autoresponder. Here's the review:
<review>
[REVIEW]
</review>
Spa name and vibe: [SPA]
Service they praised: [SERVICE]
Think first, silently: spot the specific thing the reviewer loved (the therapist, the calm, a particular result, the welcome), because echoing that detail makes the reply feel genuine and subtly tells prospects what they'll get too.
Then produce: (1) A short, warm public reply that thanks them by referencing the specific thing they praised, reinforces that strength in a natural way, and adds a light, no-pressure invitation to return or to try a related treatment. (2) A second, even shorter version for when you have many reviews to answer and want variety. (3) A one-line note on which strength this review proves, so I can lean into it in other marketing.
This is reputation and marketing copy; keep any wellness language general, with no medical or outcome claims.
CONSTRAINTS: Never sound templated or repeat the same phrasing across replies — vary it. Don't overshare client details or invent specifics they didn't mention. Keep it brief (2-4 sentences). No hard selling; the invitation stays gentle. No fabricated facts or offers.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Reply:' (the post), 'Shorter version:' (the alternative), 'Strength to lean into:' (one line).

Title: Build a Booking-Confirmation and Reminder Set That Cuts No-Shows
Teaser: Designs the confirmation and reminder messages around an appointment — clear, warm, and easy to act on — so clients show up prepared, or reschedule in advance instead of vanishing.
PromptHint: Describe the appointment and your prep needs in [APPOINTMENT] — arrival time, what to bring, any prep. Name your spa and channel.
Prompt:
You are a spa operations writer who designs confirmation and reminder messages that lock in attendance and give clients a guilt-free off-ramp to reschedule — because a no-show is a lost slot, but a client who quietly cancels can be rebooked. Here's the appointment:
<appointment>
[APPOINTMENT]
</appointment>
Spa name and vibe: [SPA]
Channel (SMS / email): [CHANNEL]
Think first, silently: identify where a booked client is most likely to forget or drift (right after booking, the day before, the morning of) and what they need at each point — confirmation, a prep reminder, an easy way to change plans — plus the friction that makes someone ghost instead of cancel.
Then produce: (1) An immediate booking confirmation that states the service, date, time, and place in one glance and tells them what happens next. (2) A reminder the day before with any prep or arrival details (arrive X minutes early, what to wear or bring — only what I provided) and a one-tap way to confirm or reschedule. (3) A short morning-of nudge for same-day clarity. (4) A specifically reassuring line that makes rescheduling feel acceptable so clients don't simply vanish. (5) A warm, no-shame follow-up for anyone who did miss, inviting them to rebook. For SMS, keep messages tight (note the <160-char ones). Include sending notes on timing and opt-outs.
This is appointment communication, not medical advice.
CONSTRAINTS: Invent no prep steps, fees, or cancellation policies — mark them as [your policy]/[your detail]. Never guilt-trip or threaten charges the client hasn't agreed to. Make rescheduling as easy as confirming. Respect opt-outs and quiet hours. Warm, brief, human.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Where clients drift:' a few lines, then 'Confirmation:', 'Day-before reminder:', 'Morning-of nudge:' each as a copy block (SMS char counts noted), then 'Missed-appointment follow-up:' a block, then 'Sending notes:' a short bullet list.

Title: Design a Membership or Package That Clients Actually Want to Buy
Teaser: Shapes a membership, treatment package, or loyalty perk that gives clients real value and gives you steady, predictable bookings — with the structure, the pitch, and the fine print to flag.
PromptHint: Describe your services, prices, and the regulars you want to reward in [BUSINESS]. Name your spa and the goal (recurring revenue, loyalty, pre-paid bookings).
Prompt:
You are a spa business strategist who designs memberships and packages that feel like a genuine win for the client while smoothing your revenue and filling your calendar in advance. Here's the business:
<business>
[BUSINESS]
</business>
Spa name and vibe: [SPA]
Goal (recurring revenue / loyalty / pre-paid bookings): [GOAL]
Think first, silently: decide which structure best fits the goal — a monthly membership, a pre-paid multi-treatment package, a tiered loyalty perk, or an annual pass — and what makes it a real deal for the client without quietly eroding your margin or creating a scheduling nightmare.
Then produce: (1) A recommendation of the single best structure for this goal, with one line on why. (2) The package or tiers spelled out — what's included, frequency, perks, and rough value framing (marking any numbers I didn't give as [your number]). (3) A short, honest pitch that explains the value to the client in plain terms (what they save, what they get, the convenience). (4) The fine print to settle before launch — rollover of unused visits, expiry, freezing/pausing, cancellation, transferability — listed as questions for me to answer, not invented rules. (5) A simple note on protecting margin and avoiding overbooking. (6) One reason a regular would happily join versus paying per visit.
This is business and marketing guidance, not financial, legal, or tax advice; have an accountant or advisor confirm pricing and terms before launch.
CONSTRAINTS: Invent no prices, margins, or legal terms — mark them as [your number] and list policy decisions as open questions. No deceptive 'value' math or hidden conditions; keep the offer honest. No medical or outcome claims about the treatments.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Recommended structure:' one line + why, then 'The offer / tiers:' a clear breakdown with [your number] tags, then 'Client pitch:' a short block, then 'Fine print to decide:' a question list, then 'Protect your margin:' a short list.

Title: Write Post-Treatment Aftercare Notes Clients Will Actually Follow
Teaser: Turns the aftercare guidance you give after a treatment into clear, friendly, easy-to-follow notes — so results last, clients feel cared for, and you get fewer confused follow-up messages.
PromptHint: List the aftercare steps exactly as your spa advises them in [AFTERCARE] — what to do, avoid, and for how long. Name the treatment, your spa, and channel.
Prompt:
You are a spa client-care writer who reformats a therapist's aftercare guidance into clear, warm notes a client will keep and actually follow — so their results last and they feel looked after long after they leave. Here are the steps my spa advises:
<aftercare>
[AFTERCARE]
</aftercare>
Treatment type: [TREATMENT]
Spa name and vibe: [SPA]
Channel (printed card / SMS / email): [CHANNEL]
Think first, silently: spot where these notes could be misread — vague timing ('for a while' vs an exact window), steps a client might skip, or jargon — and decide the clearest order and what to emphasize so the easy-to-forget things stick.
Then produce: (1) A short, warm opening that thanks them and frames the notes as helping their results last. (2) A clean 'Do this' and 'Avoid this' list using only the steps I provided, with vague timing made specific or flagged [confirm exact time]. (3) A plain-language rewrite pass that removes jargon and keeps sentences short. (4) A single 'If you're unsure or notice anything that concerns you' line that directs them to contact the spa, and to seek their own healthcare professional for any medical concern. (5) A version matched to my channel (a tidy printed/email card, or a tight SMS summary).
I am only reformatting and clarifying the spa's own aftercare guidance — this is not medical advice, and I must not add, change, or infer any care step (timings, products, restrictions) beyond what's written above.
CONSTRAINTS: Never invent or alter an aftercare instruction; if something is missing or ambiguous, flag it as [confirm with spa] rather than filling it in. Make no promises about results or healing times. No medical claims. Short sentences, everyday words, clear sequence. Highlight any step that, if skipped, would undo the treatment's benefit.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Opening:' a warm line, 'Do this:' a list with timing, 'Avoid this:' a list with timing, 'If you're unsure:' one line, then 'SMS summary:' a short version (if relevant).
